# Chapter 2 — From Crane to Crease Pattern

*Every fold is a sentence. Every model is a paragraph.*

The crane is the most famous origami model in the world. Most people who have folded paper have folded a crane. The shape — bird with a long neck, spread wings, tail at the back — has become shorthand for the entire craft. But the crane is also one of the most mathematically interesting forms in classical origami, and tracing how it came to be reveals something important about how folding evolved.

## The history nobody quite agrees on

Origami probably came from China, where paper was invented around 100 CE, but it did not become a discipline until it reached Japan in the sixth or seventh century. For most of its history it was tied to religious ceremony. Folded paper appeared in Shinto rituals, in noshi attached to gifts, in the noshikuchi attached to letters. Folding was not a craft. It was a form of attention.

The crane became central to this slowly. By the Edo period, a tradition of folding one thousand cranes — senbazuru — had formed. The thousand cranes were strung together and given as a gift for someone facing illness, or as an offering at a shrine. The number was symbolic. The folding was repetitive. The point was the time.

There is no single first crane. There is no single first folder. The model spread through families, through monasteries, through children copying their grandmothers. By the time it appeared in print — in 1797, in the *Senbazuru Orikata*, a book of instructions — it had already been folded for centuries.

## What the crane teaches

If you sit down and fold a crane from instructions, you will notice something. The first six or seven folds are not the crane. They are the bird base. The bird base is a preparation — a pattern of creases that gets the paper into a configuration from which the crane (and several other models) can be folded.

This is the secret of classical origami: every famous model is built on top of a small set of bases. The bird base. The fish base. The frog base. The waterbomb base. A skilled folder learns the bases first, then the variations. Once you know the bird base, the crane is twenty seconds of finishing work. So is the flapping bird. So is the peacock. So are dozens of others.

This is the same structure as a recipe book. The bases are mise en place. The models are the dishes.

## The crease pattern as a notation

Modern origami invented a way to write all this down. A crease pattern is the unfolded version of a model — a flat square covered in lines, with the lines marked for direction. Solid lines are mountains. Dashed lines are valleys. From the crease pattern, an experienced folder can reproduce the model without instructions.

This is not just a technical convenience. It is a language. A crease pattern is to origami what sheet music is to a symphony. Two folders looking at the same crease pattern will produce the same model. Two composers writing for the same orchestra start from the same notation. The artifact is not the performance. The artifact is the structure.

Robert Lang and others have used this notation to design models that would have been impossible by trial and error. A six-legged stag beetle, a violinist with a bow, a hundred-tentacled octopus — all designed first as crease patterns, then folded as final proofs of the math.

## A bridge to the next chapter

We have moved from a single fold to a model to an entire notation. The crane sits in the middle of this progression: famous enough to be familiar, deep enough to keep teaching. The next chapter returns to the geometry of Chapter 1 and asks: when you sit down at a square and have to make the first cut, where do you start? The answer, again, is the diagonal.
